title: practical english usage - Part 211
content:

grammatical subject. This can also move the main new information to the end - the topic - the thing we  are talking about - even though it is not the If we  begin a sentence with something else (‘ fronting’ ), this is often to make it I just can't stand people like that.

Affirmative sentences most often begin with the grammatical subject.

already        discussed.

272 fronting:     This     question         we   have 'What do you mean?' he asked.

If the subject is a pronoun, it usually comes before the verb.

'I love you,' whispered Jess.

'What do you mean?' asked Henry, (   ... Henry asked.) suggested, etc when these follow direct speech.

In story-telling, the subject often comes after reporting verbs like said, asked, 2 reporting:      'What      do   you     mean      ?' asked       Henry.

Here's those papers you wanted.

In informal speech, here's and there's often introduce plural subjects (►   130.6).

Look - there's the bus.

Here are your keys, (     Here-youiHeeys-etre!) Inversion is normal in here/there is/are ...

Here she comes, (     Here eomes she?) Off we go!

If the subject is a pronoun, it goes before the verb.

The door opened and out came Angela's boyfriend.

I stopped the car, and up walked a police officer.

There goes your brother.

Here comes Freddy! (     Here Freddy comes!) and adverb particles.

This structure is often used in speech with here, there and other short adverbs grammar •  273   cleft sentences: It was my secretary who ...

It was my secretary that sent the bill to Mr Harding yesterday.

My secretary sent the bill to Mr Harding yesterday.

be emphasised are usually joined to the relative clause by that.

In one kind of cleft sentence, we  use preparatory it is!was (►  268). The  words to 1  preparatory         it The  emphasis is often contrastive - to contradict a false belief or expectation.

for emphasis in written language), but they are also common in speech.

See also entries on basic word order (►  215), normal order and variations (►   267), ‘ spacing out* information Much  as I respect his work, I cannot agree with him.

Fast though she drove, she could not catch them.

Tired though she was, she went on working.

Young as I was, I realised what was happening.

or though (►  255).

Fronted adjectives and adverbs are possible in a structure with as 4 fronting      with    as  or  though Round the corner came Mrs Porter.

circumstances tickets can ...) not Under no circumstances can tickets be exchanged, (     Under no adverbial expressions.

Inversion (►  270-271) is necessary after some emphatic fronted adverbs and O#    we go! Down you come!

Adverb particles are often fronted when giving instructions to small children.

you can see... At the top of the stairs ...

Inside the front door there is... Opposite the living room is... On  the right that... After dark,...

Once upon a time there were three little pigs. One day... Then ... Soon after piece of narrative or a description.

uncommon in modern English.

In a few exclamatory expressions, a noun is fronted before that, but this is Last for ever these shoes will.
